title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Tonight: Locals halt Consumers' massive plant, but what does it mean for your electric bill?

lines.text: Good evening, and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Sunday night, and the big story tonight is how a rural push in western Michigan just killed a 1.4 gigawatt gas plant before it could even reach the grid.
That sounds like a big deal for everyone who pays an electric bill. Graham, walk us through what happened today.
Consumers Energy had proposed this major new natural gas plant to help meet surging demand from data centers and other large users. Residents in western Michigan organized and blocked it.
How did they block it exactly? Was this the first time something like this happened?
The utility has now withdrawn the proposal entirely after the local opposition. It's part of a broader pattern where communities are pushing back on projects tied to the data center boom.
Why does this matter for regular folks? Tighter supply sounds like it could mean higher costs.
Exactly. Without that plant, ratepayers across the Lower Peninsula face tighter electricity supply and possible higher costs as AI facilities keep demanding more power.
Data centers need a ton of electricity, right? How much are we talking about here?
A single large facility can use as much power as a small city. The 1.4 gigawatts from this plant would have helped cover that growth, but now the grid has to find alternatives.
What about water and farmland? These plants and data centers both pull resources from rural areas.
That's the core of the fight. Residents are worried about impacts on local water supplies, farmland conversion, and the character of their towns. The gas plant would have added to those pressures.
Who are the main players here besides Consumers and the residents?

Hyperscale developers are driving the demand, local townships are fielding proposals, and state lawmakers are starting to look at new rules. It's a classic tension between economic development and local control.
What happens next if this plant is off the table? Do we just wait for blackouts or rate hikes?
The utility will have to look at other options, but in the short term analysts expect tighter supply margins. That could translate to higher bills this winter and beyond.
Is there any history of similar pushback in Michigan before today?
We've seen townships across the state pause or reject projects in recent months. This Consumers withdrawal shows the opposition has real teeth when it mobilizes.
How does this fit with the other stories we're seeing today about tax breaks and moratoriums?
It all points to the same pressure cooker. Communities are using every tool — protests, zoning pauses, and now killing generation projects — to slow the pace.
Graham, do you think this changes the timeline for data center approvals going forward?
It likely does. Developers may face longer reviews and more conditions as utilities and locals recalibrate to the new reality.
Thanks for laying that out. Let's move to some quick hits from around the state.
State lawmakers are eyeing new guardrails for data centers. Bills would set stricter rules on power use and tax incentives affecting every township with proposals.
That could shift power from local boards to the state. What kind of timeline are we looking at?
It's still early in the legislative process, but passage would mean more oversight before any big project moves ahead.
In Lansing, council will consider a six-month data center pause on Monday after a $120 million project was withdrawn.
Residents pushed back hard there too. The moratorium would give time to update zoning rules.
Consumers is also warning that rates could rise if their tax fight with the state drags on. They're paying millions in disputed taxes that might get passed to customers.
The dispute is over how data-center projects are valued for property taxes. Any increase would hit households across the Lower Peninsula this winter.
And secret tax breaks for data centers have already cost the state $13 million. Those incentives stay hidden while draining local services.
Taxpayers foot the bill without knowing which projects benefit. That's adding fuel to the transparency debate.
Yesterday Bay County froze all new data center proposals with a temporary moratorium to study power and water impacts.
Residents there could face higher utility bills if large facilities strain the grid during peak summer demand.
Garfield Township planners also backed a freeze option while residents debate impacts on power and land use in northwest Michigan.
If adopted, it would pause development in Grand Traverse County until rules are in place.
In Saline Township the data center project has swelled to $16 billion with financing now locked in.
That scale rivals the largest U.S. campuses and will shape Washtenaw County growth for years through tax incentives and power demand.
Solon Township shelved rules meant to curb data centers on Saturday, leaving residents with continued uncertainty.
The board delayed an ordinance that would have restricted where large facilities could locate.
And the carpenters union threw support behind the STREAM data center build, signaling hundreds of construction jobs.
Local workers would gain steady paychecks, but towns would still face new demands on roads, water, and electricity.
Now let's look ahead to meetings listeners can actually attend.
Tomorrow Lansing City Council holds a public hearing on a proposed six-month moratorium on new data center permits and zoning applications.
Also on Monday, Solon Township Board will take up extension of its data center moratorium and related ordinances.
Lenox Township Board of Trustees meets to discuss data center ordinances, the moratorium, and related development proposals.
Lansing City Council Development & Planning has another public hearing on the 182-day data center moratorium.
And on Tuesday Saline Township Board of Trustees holds a special meeting to consider an Industrial Facilities Tax Exemption Certificate for Oracle's $43 billion data center project.
Plenty for residents to weigh in on if they want their voices heard.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.
Thanks for listening tonight. Stay informed on these changes coming to communities across Michigan.

episode_title: Rural Residents Block 1.4 GW Gas Plant — Power Bills and Data Center Growth at Stake
